subtopics.description: Deliverable-first week mapping: identify what the participant delivered or completed last week, then work backwards to discover the underlying tasks — building the base task inventory collaboratively.
coverage_criteria: STEP 1 (goals first) — REQUIRED: Ask 'What were your goals for the last week?' or 'What were you trying to get done last week?' This anchors the conversation in intent before outcomes. The participant has stated at least one goal or priority they had in mind for the week. A broad answer ('finish a draft', 'prep for a meeting', 'make progress on X') is sufficient.

STEP 2 (deliverable probe) — REQUIRED: Once goals are on the table, probe what actually got done: 'And what did you actually deliver or complete?' This surfaces the gap or alignment between intent and outcome — both are useful. The participant has named at least one concrete output or completed item: something they finished, submitted, presented, sent, or handed off.

STEP 3 (task extraction from deliverables) — REQUIRED: For each deliverable named, probe backwards with 'What did you need to do to get that done?' Tasks emerge naturally from this. Hook follow-ups directly to what they said: 'You mentioned [specific deliverable] — what do you usually need to do before or after that?' At least one deliverable has been traced back to at least one clear action+object task. Do NOT ask generic structural questions (e.g., 'walk me through a typical day') after deliverables have been named — that's the same information goal in disguise.

STEP 4 (instance-to-pattern check) — REQUIRED when relevant: For any deliverable that sounds one-off, confirm whether it recurs: 'Is that something you do regularly, or was that a one-time thing?' Confirmed recurring deliverables and their underlying tasks form the base inventory. One-off items are noted but not anchored as recurring tasks.

Every task surfaced should have at least two components: an ACTION (what the participant does) and an OBJECT (what they act on). An OBJECTIVE (why) is optional. The interviewer MUST attempt to infer missing details from prior context before asking. Ask about objective only when it is genuinely needed to disambiguate the task, and at most once.

subtopics.description: Task List Completion: sweeping for lower-cadence tasks, confirming the list is exhaustive, and ensuring each task has a clear action and object (objective optional).
coverage_criteria: STEP 2 (frequency sweep) — REQUIRED: Ask exactly one cadence-specific question about lower-frequency work (monthly/quarterly/few-times-a-year). The question must reference cadence/frequency directly. Do NOT use a generic catch-all like 'anything else?' as the primary wording.

STEP 3 (closure rule) — CONDITIONAL after STEP 2: If the participant gives a clear no-new-work response (e.g., 'no', 'that's it', 'nothing else', 'just that'), mark this subtopic complete immediately. Do NOT ask another catch-all, completeness, or rephrased variant. Also treat explicit repetition/frustration signals with no new tasks (e.g., 'you keep asking this', 'am I not giving enough detail?') as closure and move on.

COMPLETION — this subtopic is DONE when the most recent cadence sweep/closure response adds no new tasks. If the participant introduces new work, capture clear action/object (and objective when available) for the new task(s), then ask one final completion confirmation only if needed.
If the user gives two consecutive sub-5-word replies with no new task information, stop probing and treat the completeness confirmation as passed.

Every task in the inventory should have at least two components: an ACTION (what the participant does) and an OBJECT (what they act on). An OBJECTIVE (why — the goal or outcome) is helpful but optional. The interviewer MUST attempt to infer missing details from prior context before asking. If action+object are clear, treat the task as covered even when purpose is broad, variable, or hard to articulate (e.g., 'it depends', 'to answer their question'). Ask about objective only when it is genuinely needed to disambiguate the task, and ask at most once.

subtopics.description: Time allocation: how much time they spend on each task, as a percentage of their total work week. A time-split widget is shown to the participant to collect the actual percentages. IMPORTANT: The interviewer MUST send a message targeting this subtopic — for example, 'Now let's see how your time breaks down across these tasks — here's a quick widget for you to fill in.' This message triggers the widget. Do NOT ask for per-task percentages one by one in conversation. Do NOT skip this subtopic even if task-list completeness was already confirmed in the Breadth subtopic.
coverage_criteria: PREREQUISITE: The task list has been confirmed complete per the COMPLETION criterion of the Task List Completion subtopic — i.e. the most recent completeness confirmation was answered without introducing any new tasks. Do NOT proceed to this subtopic if the participant's last reply to a completeness question introduced new work. If the task list is not yet confirmed complete, ask the completeness question first.

The participant has provided explicit percentage or fraction breakdowns showing how their work time is divided across tasks — for example, 'I spend about 50% on X and 30% on Y', or a submitted widget with per-task percentages. Evaluate this criterion by scanning the conversation for explicit numeric time splits: if NO percentages or fractions appear in the participant's messages, this criterion is FALSE regardless of any other confirmations. A 'yes' or 'no that's it' reply to a completeness question does NOT satisfy this criterion.

Grouped categories are valid for time allocation (the split does NOT need to map 1:1 to every Task Inventory line item). If the provided percentages plausibly cover a full typical week (approximately 95-105% total, or explicitly stated as complete), mark this subtopic complete and move on immediately. A single task at 100% is a valid complete split — do NOT ask for sub-task or sub-category breakdowns.

Do NOT ask post-split follow-up questions in this subtopic once a plausible breakdown is provided. Specifically avoid representativeness/completeness prompts like 'does that breakdown feel representative of most weeks?' or 'what isn't captured there?', and NEVER ask the participant to break a task down further into sub-tasks or sub-categories. Record the split and move on immediately. Only if a required number is missing or inconsistent may you ask one targeted clarification about that specific gap.

subtopics.description: Priority tasks: identify which task(s) matter most and ensure each priority task is specific.
coverage_criteria: STEP 1 (open ask) — REQUIRED: Ask once, open-endedly, which task feels most important (e.g., 'Which of those feels most important to you?' or 'Which one matters most?'). If the participant already named a clear priority earlier (including high-stakes framing like 'presenting and writing are high stakes'), treat STEP 1 as satisfied without asking again. Do NOT ask a yes/no confirmation variant ('Would you say that's your main priority?') — restating their answer as a check is a duplicate.

STEP 2 (specificity sweep) — REQUIRED only for priority tasks not already clarified: Scan every priority task named in STEP 1. For any task whose action and object were already made specific earlier in this conversation (e.g., during deliverable-first task extraction), treat it as already covered — do NOT re-ask. Only ask a clarifying question for tasks that still have a vague action ('do', 'handle', 'work on', 'deal with') or a vague object ('analysis', 'meetings', 'emails', 'documents', 'work', 'projects', 'research' without a concrete qualifier) AND where that vagueness was not already resolved. One clarification per vague task, one per turn. If the participant answers with 'all of the above', 'all of it', 'everything', 'all three', or any equivalent — that IS a complete answer: the object is the entire thing they named. Accept it immediately and move on. Do NOT rephrase the same clarifying question. Cap at 1 clarification attempt per task regardless of answer quality — if the participant engaged with the question at all, accept their answer and move on.
COMPLETION: Once STEP 1 is answered and STEP 2's sweep is done (or all priority tasks are already specific), this subtopic is DONE. Transition to Time Allocation.

description: Priority tasks: identify which task(s) matter most, ensure each priority task is specific, and capture a workflow description for the priority task(s).
coverage_criteria: STEP 1 (open ask) — REQUIRED: Ask once, open-endedly, which task feels most important (e.g., 'Which of those feels most important to you?' or 'Which one matters most?'). If the participant already named a clear priority earlier (including high-stakes framing like 'presenting and writing are high stakes'), treat STEP 1 as satisfied without asking again. Do NOT ask a yes/no confirmation variant ('Would you say that's your main priority?') — restating their answer as a check is a duplicate.

STEP 2 (specificity sweep) — REQUIRED: Scan every priority task. For any task with a vague action ('do', 'handle', 'work on', 'deal with') or a vague object ('analysis', 'meetings', 'emails', 'documents', 'work', 'projects', 'research' without a concrete qualifier), ask ONE targeted clarifying question per task to pin down the missing specific (e.g., 'Analysis of what — experiment results, interview data?'; 'Meetings with whom, about what?'). One clarification per turn. Cap total sweep clarifications at 3 — if the participant's answer is still vague after one attempt, accept it and move on.

STEP 3 (workflow capture) — REQUIRED for priority task(s) only: Ask exactly this two-sentence structure, nothing more: 'Think back to the last time you did <priority task>. Walk me through the steps you took.' Do NOT paraphrase. Do NOT add a presupposed start or end state (e.g., 'from blank page to finished draft', 'from I need a plan to having it written down') — that is leading and confusing. Do NOT double-barrel it. Accept whatever the participant gives — do NOT ask follow-up workflow questions even if the response seems incomplete. One question per priority task. Only apply this step to task(s) named as priority in STEP 1 — do NOT drill workflow for non-priority tasks.

COMPLETION: Once STEP 1 is answered, STEP 2's sweep is done (or no vague priority tasks remain), and STEP 3 has captured a workflow for each priority task, this subtopic is DONE. Transition to Time Allocation.

subtopics.description: Their role or title
coverage_criteria: Provides their current role or title, such as job title, academic position, or equivalent.

description: Tasks done with AI tools, new tasks created by AI, and resulting workflow changes
coverage_criteria: Distinguishes among (a) tasks still done manually, (b) existing tasks now done with AI tools, and (c) net-new tasks that exist because AI tools are available.
For each task done with AI tools (including net-new AI-created tasks), capture how often AI is used for that task.
For each AI-used existing task, capture at least one concrete workflow change versus before AI (what step changed, what was removed/added, or what became faster/slower).
For each net-new AI-created task, capture what made it possible/necessary and whether it replaced, expanded, or added to prior responsibilities.

The Interviewer has explicitly probed manual-task AI demand: for tasks still mostly manual, ask whether there is any part they wish AI could help with but currently do not use a tool for. Capture either (a) at least one desired-but-not-yet-adopted AI use case, or (b) an explicit 'none/no interest' answer.
If a desired-but-not-yet-adopted AI use case is identified, capture the main adoption blocker (e.g., quality/trust, privacy/policy, setup friction, cost, lack of fit).

ANTI-DUPLICATION: Do NOT ask two near-duplicate workflow-difference questions in a row for the same task set. If the participant already gave a clear global answer (e.g., 'not very different', 'mostly the same', 'completely different'), treat the workflow-change question as answered and move to remaining missing dimensions.
ANTI-DUPLICATION: Once AI-usage frequency is answered (e.g., 'every time', 'occasionally'), do not re-ask frequency with different wording for the same tasks unless the prior answer was ambiguous.

description: Priority tasks: the task(s) the participant considers most important in their work.
coverage_criteria: STEP 1 (open ask) — REQUIRED: The Interviewer has asked the participant, open-endedly, which task feels most important to them (e.g., 'Which of those feels most important to you?' or 'Which one matters most?'). Ask this once regardless of how many priorities the participant names.

STEP 2 (direct confirmation) — REQUIRED only when needed: Confirm main priority once with direct wording (e.g., 'Would you say that's your main priority right now?'). Do NOT use 'anything else on the same level' phrasing. If the participant already gave an explicit singular answer (e.g., 'just the report', 'that's the main one'), treat STEP 2 as satisfied without asking another question.

COMPLETION: Once STEP 1 is answered and STEP 2 is either asked once or implicitly satisfied by a clear singular answer, this subtopic is DONE. Mark it covered and move on immediately. Do NOT ask any further questions about priorities.
For each priority task, map out the workflow of the task and the subtasks that are part of the workflow.

description: Priority tasks: the task(s) the participant considers most important in their work.
coverage_criteria: STEP 1 (open ask): Ask once, open-ended, which task is most important — e.g., 'Which of those feels most important to you?' or 'Which one matters most?'. Do not frame as 'one or two'; let the participant name whatever count they volunteer.

STEP 2 (direct confirmation): If needed, ask ONE direct confirmation (e.g., 'Would you say that's your main priority right now?'). Do NOT ask 'anything else on the same level' variants. If the user already states a single clear priority, do not ask a second confirmation.
Once STEP 1 is answered and STEP 2 is either asked once or implicitly satisfied by a clear singular answer, mark this subtopic covered and advance.
Distinguishes priority from time-spent — a task can be low-frequency but high-stakes.

Do NOT plant reasons or offer multiple-choice framings when asking about priority (e.g., avoid 'is that because of X, or because of Y?'). Use open probes: 'What makes it the most important?' or 'Why that one?'.

Questions in this subtopic must NOT include examples, suggestions, or option lists (no 'like reading, coding, writing, meetings', 'such as X', 'for example'). Ask open-endedly — let the participant name the task in their own words.

description: Tasks done with AI tools, new tasks created by AI, and resulting workflow changes
coverage_criteria: Distinguishes among (a) tasks still done manually, (b) existing tasks now done with AI tools, and (c) net-new tasks that exist because AI tools are available.
For each task done with AI tools (including net-new AI-created tasks), capture how often AI is used for that task.
For each net-new AI-created task, capture what made it possible/necessary and whether it replaced, expanded, or added to prior responsibilities.
For each task done with AI tools, capture how workflow changed versus pre-AI (steps added/removed, speed, quality control/review burden, and handoffs).

ANTI-DUPLICATION: Do NOT ask two near-duplicate workflow-difference questions in a row for the same task set. If the participant already gave a clear global answer (e.g., 'not very different', 'mostly the same', 'completely different'), treat the workflow-change question as answered and move to remaining missing dimensions.
ANTI-DUPLICATION: Once AI-usage frequency is answered (e.g., 'every time', 'occasionally'), do not re-ask frequency with different wording for the same tasks unless the prior answer was ambiguous.

subtopics.description: Skills required for their priority tasks
coverage_criteria: For each priority task they named, identifies at least one key skill required to perform it.
Skills are specific capabilities (e.g., 'statistical modeling', 'stakeholder negotiation'), NOT broad traits (e.g., 'analytical thinking', 'communication').
Do NOT ask about skills for every task — focus only on their priority tasks.

subtopics.description: Skills that differentiate them from others in their profession
coverage_criteria: Names skills they have that are rare or unusual for someone in their role or field.
Distinguishes from general competencies that everyone in their profession has.

subtopics.description: What the person actually does — the core verb or activity at the center of the task.
coverage_criteria: The respondent names a specific, observable action rather than a role label or end goal.
The action is scoped to a meaningful unit of work — neither a single click nor an entire job function.

subtopics.description: What the action is performed on, to, or for — the primary target that gives the action its specificity.
coverage_criteria: The respondent identifies a concrete target (document, case, dataset, system, person, product, etc.).
The target is specific enough to distinguish this task from other tasks using the same verb.

subtopics.description: Why the task is performed — the immediate result or objective it produces, not a downstream business outcome.
coverage_criteria: The respondent states a direct, proximate purpose rather than only describing what they do.
The purpose distinguishes this task from other tasks involving the same action-object pair.

subtopics.description: What the person uses to carry out the task — software, equipment, instruments, or materials, as distinct from what the task is performed on or what guides it.
coverage_criteria: The respondent names at least one tool, system, or material central to execution.
Tools are identified specifically enough to be meaningful (e.g., a named platform, not just 'software').

subtopics.description: Where guidance, inputs, or reference material comes from — the policies, procedures, knowledge, briefs, data feeds, prior cases, or people the worker draws on.
coverage_criteria: The respondent identifies at least one source of input or guidance that shapes how the task is done.
The response indicates the degree of prescription: tightly scripted, loosely guided, or largely self-directed.

subtopics.description: How the task is carried out — the process, sequence, or technique, when that detail is necessary to understand the work.
coverage_criteria: The respondent describes a process or technique beyond simply restating the action, object, and purpose.
The method detail is sufficient to distinguish the respondent's approach from other possible approaches.

subtopics.description: Where the worker exercises discretion — any point requiring evaluation, comparison, prioritization, diagnosis, or selection, and the basis for that decision.
coverage_criteria: The respondent identifies at least one decision point within the task.
The respondent names the factors, criteria, or signals used to make that judgment.
The response makes clear whether the worker is exercising discretion or simply applying a fixed rule.

subtopics.description: What counts as done and done well — the standards, checks, thresholds, or completion conditions used to evaluate the output.
coverage_criteria: At least one concrete standard, check, or threshold is named rather than a vague appeal to quality.

subtopics.description: The surrounding conditions that shape execution, time pressure, coordination dependencies, regulatory constraints, physical setting, or system limitations, included only when they materially affect how the task is performed.
coverage_criteria: The respondent identifies at least one contextual factor that concretely changes how the task is done.
The factor is tied to its effect on the task rather than stated for background details.

subtopics.description: How often the task is performed.
coverage_criteria: The respondent indicates whether the task is performed daily, weekly, monthly, seasonally, ad hoc, or triggered by specific events.
The answer is specific enough to distinguish routine tasks from infrequent or exceptional ones.

subtopics.description: How much time the task takes, either per instance or as a share of total work time.
coverage_criteria: The respondent provides an approximate duration per instance, per cycle, or as a percentage of time spent.

subtopics.description: Skills and developed capacities the worker draws on to perform the task.
coverage_criteria: The respondent identifies at least one concrete skill, knowledge area, or capability used in carrying out the task.
The skill is tied to the task itself rather than described only as a general trait or qualification.
The answer makes clear whether the skill is essential to execution, helpful but secondary, or mainly relevant at decision points.

subtopics.description: Prior work-related experience typically needed to perform the task competently.
coverage_criteria: The respondent indicates whether prior experience is usually needed before someone can perform the task well.
The answer describes the kind or approximate amount of related experience that helps a worker perform the task competently or with less supervision.
The response makes clear that experience refers to prior exposure or practice, not the skill itself or the training used to learn it.

subtopics.description: Training and preparation needed to learn to perform the task, including formal courses, certifications, onboarding, mentorship, or self-directed learning.
coverage_criteria: The respondent identifies at least one kind of training, instruction, or guided preparation used to learn the task.
The answer indicates whether the task can be learned quickly on the job or requires substantial formal or supervised training.
